impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's practice of conditioning patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court held that the jury properly determined the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense against antitrust liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of anticompetitive tying arrangements may still defend by showing the tied product lacks substantial non-infringing uses, which remains a viable defense when properly established.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk where those products have uses outside the patent's scope, even if the patent is later invalidated.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages based on foreign sales were properly awarded.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the judgment, holding that Columbia's asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages for foreign sales. The decision involved a $185 million jury verdict for willful infringement of computer virus detection patents.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in patent cases involving software and cybersecurity patents gain a stronger basis to challenge eligibility under § 101, and companies selling software globally may limit damages exposure for foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with software-related inventions, including cybersecurity technologies, face heightened risk that claims will be found abstract at Alice step one, potentially invalidating significant patent portfolios.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims covering server-side tailoring of content specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are eligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patent claims were ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment on Netflix's cross-appeal. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities. This reversed a jury verdict finding Netflix infringed the '715 patent and a $2.5 million damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing infringement suits involving content adaptation and streaming technology patents can leverage this decision to challenge patent eligibility under Alice.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to server-side content optimization for different device capabilities face substantial eligibility challenges, undermining the enforceability of such patents.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was proper on claims of inequitable conduct in patent procurement and Walker Process antitrust fraud for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves Tenaris's coiled tubing patents and allegations that Tenaris concealed prior CYMAX technology from the PTO, potentially constituting fraud sufficient to support antitrust liability for attempted monopolization.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud can no longer rely on summary judgment where factual disputes exist about intent to deceive the PTO.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk where competitors allege that prior art was concealed during prosecution, as both inequitable conduct and antitrust fraud claims will proceed to trial when material facts are disputed.

impacts.caseName: REGENXBIO Inc. v. Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to cultured host cells containing recombinant nucleic acid molecules encoding an AAV capsid protein are ineligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101 as directed to a natural phenomenon.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's summary judgment of patent ineligibility, holding that claims to engineered host cells containing recombinant AAV sequences are not directed to a natural phenomenon. The decision is significant for the gene therapy industry, as it preserves patent protection for engineered biological constructs used in gene therapy vector production.
impacts.positiveImplications: Biotech and pharmaceutical companies developing gene therapies gain critical assurance that patent claims to engineered biological materials—such as host cells with recombinant viral sequences—are eligible for patent protection under § 101.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies seeking to develop competing gene therapy products face continued patent barriers for engineered biological constructs, as such claims survive natural phenomenon challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review petitions are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions on discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking. The court reasoned that the Director retains unreviewable discretion over IPR institution, the instructions do not bind the Director, and non-institution authority is insulated from judicial review. This upholds the Director's ability to issue guidance limiting IPR institution without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO Director's continued broad discretion to deny IPR institution, potentially reducing challenges to issued patents through administrative proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on IPR as a cost-effective alternative to district court litigation for challenging patents face continued uncertainty, as Director guidance limiting IPR institution is insulated from both rulemaking requirements and judicial review.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were permissible.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class action settlement resolving claims that PACER fees exceeded statutory authority under the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, rejecting a pro se objector's challenges to jurisdiction, settlement fairness, and the propriety of incentive awards.
impacts.positiveImplications: The decision validates the use of class actions to challenge excessive government fees and confirms that incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives remain permissible, supporting institutional plaintiffs in public interest litigation.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC properly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 and upheld the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watch models. The court found no error in the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings regarding Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygenation measurement technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with innovative wearable technology patents have a powerful enforcement mechanism through ITC exclusion orders that can block importation of infringing products by even the largest technology companies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major technology companies integrating patented health-monitoring features into consumer electronics face significant supply chain disruption risk from ITC exclusion orders, potentially requiring product redesigns or removal of key features.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute, and whether 19 U.S.C. § 1516a abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute. The court also held that § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial proceedings. The decision requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than apply automatic designation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies participating in trade remedy proceedings as interested parties or competitors gain greater access to information submitted during investigations, potentially improving transparency and the ability to challenge ITC determinations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting business-sensitive data in antidumping and countervailing duty questionnaires face increased risk of disclosure, as the ITC can no longer automatically designate all responses as confidential and must instead make case-by-case determinations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report, denying reopening of discovery, and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated the summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The case involves patents related to virtual network optimization technology used in Microsoft's Azure platform. The court held the expert exclusion was an abuse of discretion, requiring the case to proceed on damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more forgiving standard for damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that technical methodological disputes will result in complete exclusion of damages evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Large technology companies defending patent suits face reduced ability to obtain summary judgment by challenging damages methodology, as courts must give plaintiffs greater latitude in presenting damages theories.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding two expert witnesses on induced patent infringement and in granting judgment as a matter of law to the defendant in a medical device patent case.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of both expert witnesses and JMOL for DePuy, remanding for a new trial. The case involves patents on spinal surgery derotation techniques. The court held the exclusions were an abuse of discretion, which improperly deprived the plaintiff of his ability to prove induced infringement by surgeons using DePuy's medical devices.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting induced infringement claims against medical device manufacturers have greater ability to present expert testimony on how physicians use accused products, even where claim constructions are broad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face increased induced infringement exposure, as courts must allow broader expert testimony on how surgeons use devices in configurations that may infringe method patents.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a mandatory, money-mandating obligation requiring the SBA to award grants to eligible applicants in the order applications were received, supporting Tucker Act jurisdiction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the RRF statute's use of 'shall award grants' and 'in the order in which applications are received' created mandatory obligations supporting Tucker Act jurisdiction over claims by restaurants denied grants. Over 300 restaurants sued for unpaid RRF grants after the SBA processed applications out of order, and the court allowed their claims to proceed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses and restaurants that applied for but did not receive RRF grants may pursue monetary claims against the government, potentially recovering grant amounts they were statutorily entitled to receive.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability to hundreds of applicants who were passed over despite applying earlier, establishing a precedent that statutory grant programs with mandatory language create enforceable monetary obligations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a means-plus-function claim limitation ('control means') in a crop harvester patent was indefinite where the specification described the corresponding structure using general terms like 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted patent claims were invalid as indefinite and remanded for further proceedings. The court held that the specification's description of the 'control means' structure—including a controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system—was sufficient to inform a person of ordinary skill, even though the specification described some components as 'conventional.'
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more permissive standard for definiteness, as specifications that describe corresponding structures in general terms may still satisfy the definiteness requirement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies defending against means-plus-function patent claims face a higher bar for invalidating such claims on indefiniteness grounds, even when specifications use broad or general descriptions of corresponding structures.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims covering web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of the patent infringement complaint for failure to state a claim, finding the web conferencing patent claims ineligible under § 101. The claims covered systems allowing participants to observe conferencing sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion at different playback rates. This decision reinforces the application of Alice to software-based communication and collaboration technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and streaming platforms can more confidently defend against patent assertions involving basic time-shifting and asynchronous viewing functionalities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to web conferencing features like time-shifting, asynchronous playback, and multi-stream management face significant § 101 eligibility challenges.
